prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response:
"Force Ten" by Rush: 

This song, from Rush's 1987 album "Hold Your Fire," chokes me up every time. The lyrics, penned by drummer Neil Peart, are a poignant tribute to his friend and former band member Alex Lifeson's parents, who died in a tragic auto accident. Accounts indicate that Alex was in the car but miraculously survived.

What gets me is how the song weaves together an almost achingly beautiful melody (that guitar arpeggio intro alone is sublime) with a gambit of emotions. There's a buoyancy in Geddy Lee's soaring vocals that captures both the resilience and fragility of life through a dark event. The track shifts through gears -- a soft opening, a driving middle, a hypnotic solo section --  each one echoing the turbulence and profound effect of grief.

But the coup is how the song transforms. Peart's drumming pounds and builds till it breaks like a shockwave, yet each time, the luminous duet of Geddy Lee's voice and Alex Lifeson's guitar re-establishes that through-it-all steady heartbeat. It pulls together hurt and resolve, loss and memory, chaos and comfort, not just on the surface but really getting into the stuff of the song, organically. Every listen is an emotional rollercoaster, but a meaningful one.  It never fails to make me think of my own experiences with loss and the way love and bond don't break but get to pass through the next door along with the sorrow.
